  Introduction


An in-depth analysis of the Free Mermaid Live Editor, exploring its technical capabilities and implications for web development.


  
  
  Understanding the Free Mermaid Live Editor: A Technical Overview


The Free Mermaid Live Editor is a powerful web-based tool designed for creating diagrams quickly and efficiently. It allows users to create various types of diagrams, including flowcharts, sequence diagrams, and Gantt charts, using a simple markdown-like syntax. The editor runs directly in the browser, which means there’s no need to install additional software. This accessibility has made it a popular choice for developers and project managers alike.

According to its official source, the editor supports real-time collaborative editing, allowing multiple users to work on a diagram simultaneously. This feature is crucial in today’s fast-paced development environments where teams often work remotely or across different locations.

[INTERNAL:collaborative-tools|Exploring collaborative tools for remote teams]


  
  
  How Does It Work?


The underlying mechanism of the Mermaid Live Editor is based on a lightweight JavaScript library that interprets the diagram syntax into visual representations. Users input text commands that define shapes, arrows, and connections, which the editor then renders as a diagram in real time. This process involves parsing the textual data and converting it into an SVG format for high-quality output.


  
  
  Key Components:




Diagram Types: Supports various diagrams like ER diagrams, class diagrams, and mind maps.

Export Options: Users can easily export their diagrams in SVG or PNG formats for use in presentations or documentation.

Syntax: The simplicity of the syntax allows even non-technical users to create diagrams without extensive training.



  
  
  The Importance of Diagramming Tools in Web Development


In web development, effective communication is vital. The Free Mermaid Live Editor addresses this by providing a means to visualize complex ideas clearly and succinctly. Diagrams serve as a universal language that helps bridge gaps between technical and non-technical team members.


  
  
  Why Is It Important?




Enhanced Clarity: Visuals can simplify complex processes and make them more understandable.

Streamlined Workflows: By incorporating diagramming directly into development workflows, teams can identify bottlenecks and optimize processes.

Reduced Errors: A well-documented process through diagrams can lead to fewer misunderstandings and errors during development.



  
  
  Real Impact on Technology


With the rise of Agile methodologies, where iterative development and constant feedback are crucial, tools like the Mermaid Live Editor become indispensable. They provide immediate visual feedback that can help teams pivot quickly based on project needs.

The versatility of the Free Mermaid Live Editor allows it to be applied across various industries and scenarios. Here are some concrete use cases:

Project Management: Teams can create Gantt charts to track project timelines and progress.

Software Development: Developers can use flowcharts to outline algorithms or workflows.

Education: Educators can create mind maps to illustrate complex subjects or concepts during lessons.

Business Analysis: Business analysts can visualize processes for stakeholder presentations.



  
  
  Industry Applications:




Technology: Often used in software development cycles to map out system architecture.

Education: Facilitates learning by visually representing information.

Healthcare: Helps illustrate patient flows or treatment pathways.



  
  
  Comparing Mermaid Live Editor with Alternative Technologies


When assessing diagramming tools, it’s essential to understand how they stack up against alternatives. While there are several diagramming tools available, the Free Mermaid Live Editor stands out for its simplicity and accessibility.


  
  
  Alternatives Include:




Lucidchart: A robust tool but often requires a subscription for advanced features.

Draw.io: Offers similar functionalities but may not provide real-time collaboration as seamlessly as Mermaid.

Visio: A powerful option mainly used in enterprise settings but comes with a steeper learning curve and cost.



  
  
  Why Choose Mermaid?


The primary advantage of the Free Mermaid Live Editor is its lightweight nature and ease of use, which can significantly lower the barrier to entry for teams new to diagramming.


  
  
  What Does This Mean for Your Business?


In Colombia and Spain, the adoption of effective diagramming tools like the Free Mermaid Live Editor can greatly impact productivity. Teams that embrace visual collaboration tend to see improvements in communication and efficiency.


  
  
  Contextual Insights:

Time Savings: Quick diagram creation allows teams to focus on development rather than spending excessive time on documentation.

title: How to Use You.com for Ai Search Visibility Tracking in 2026
title_hi: How to Use You.com for Ai Search Visibility Tracking in 2026
summary:
Originally published at https://seointent.com/blog/you-com-for-ai-search-visibility-tracking


  
  
  TL;DR



- You.co
content:
Originally published at https://seointent.com/blog/you-com-for-ai-search-visibility-tracking


  
  
  TL;DR



- You.com for AI search visibility tracking lets you run structured prompts against a live AI search engine to see whether your brand surfaces in generated answers — without paying for enterprise tooling.

- The five-step workflow takes under 30 minutes to set up and produces repeatable, comparable snapshots you can log weekly.

- You.com's real-time web access makes it more reliable than static LLMs for brand mention tracking — though it still needs human interpretation.

- If you want to skip the manual prompting, SEOintent automates the whole process and tracks changes over time across multiple AI engines.




You.com for AI search visibility tracking is the practice of using You.com's AI-powered search interface — which combines live web retrieval with large language model reasoning — to audit whether your brand, product, or content appears in AI-generated answers to relevant queries. It gives SEOs a free, repeatable way to measure AI search presence without proprietary tools.

People are searching this right now because traditional rank tracking is becoming less relevant. If ChatGPT or Perplexity answers a buyer's question without listing your brand, you've lost the click before the SERP even loads. Tools like Perplexity and Semrush's AI overview tracker do parts of this well — Perplexity has clean source citations, Semrush has scale — but neither is designed for the hands-on prompt testing that reveals why you're absent from AI answers. This article gives you a concrete workflow, real prompt examples, and an honest comparison. If you're building out a broader content operation, the programmatic SEO guide is worth reading alongside this.


  
  
  What is You.Com For AI Search Visibility Tracking?


You.Com for AI search visibility tracking is a manual or semi-automated auditing method where you submit structured queries into You.com's AI chat interface to determine whether your brand or content is cited in AI-generated responses. It matters because AI search engines are increasingly the first answer layer users see.

Unlike running prompts through a closed LLM like ChatGPT (OpenAI), You.com pulls live search results into its responses. That means the citations you see reflect actual indexing and authority signals, not just training data from months ago. This is what makes it a useful you.com SEO tool — the gap between what you expect to rank for and what the AI actually surfaces is much smaller than with offline models. It also makes results easier to act on, since you can tie changes back to indexation, backlinks, and on-page signals.


  
  
  Why Use You.com for AI Search Visibility Tracking Specifically?


You.com earns its place in this workflow because it combines real-time web retrieval with LLM synthesis, which means you're testing actual current visibility — not a cached training snapshot. It's free at the base tier, it shows source citations inline, and it runs fast enough to test dozens of queries in a single session. For teams doing using AI for AI search visibility tracking at scale, that combination is hard to beat without spending money.


- Live web grounding — You.com retrieves current pages before generating answers, so a brand mention (or absence) reflects today's index, not data from six months ago. This makes week-over-week comparisons meaningful.

- Inline source citations — Every AI answer shows the URLs it drew from. You can immediately see if your domain appears, which page it cites, and whether it's positioned as a primary or supporting source. Check the track AI search mentions guide for how to log these systematically.

- Free tier with no rate limits for manual use — You don't need an API key to start. You can run 20-30 visibility prompts in a session without hitting a wall, which matters when you're auditing an entire topic cluster.

- Customisable AI mode — You.com lets you switch models (including GPT-4 class and Claude-class options), so you can cross-check whether your visibility differs by underlying model — a signal that tells you whether the gap is a content quality issue or a citation/indexing issue.




  
  
  How to Use You.com for AI Search Visibility Tracking: A 5-Step Workflow


The full workflow runs in five steps: define your target queries, set up a tracking log, run structured prompts in You.com, record citation data, and identify gaps for action. You'll need a spreadsheet, your target keyword list, and about 20-30 minutes for the first session. The step that trips most people up is Step 2 — vague logging makes the data useless when you return to it a week later.

- Step 1: Build your target query list. Start with 10-15 informational and commercial queries your ideal customer would ask an AI search engine. Don't just use your branded terms — use category queries where you want to appear. For example, if you sell project management software, include queries like "best project management tools for agencies 2026." Keep these in a spreadsheet with columns for query, date, model used, and citation result.

- Step 2: Set up a repeatable logging structure. Before you run a single prompt, build the log. Your columns should be: Query | Date | You.com Model | Your Domain Cited? (Y/N) | Position in Citations | Competing Domains Cited | Notes. Use a consistent date format. This structure is what turns a one-off experiment into an automated AI search visibility tracking system. The prompt you'll use for each query is: List the top tools/resources/guides for [topic] and explain why each one is recommended. Cite your sources.

- Step 3: Run the visibility audit prompts. In You.com's AI chat, paste each query from your list. Use this exact prompt format for brand/content visibility: You are helping a researcher audit which brands are most cited for [your topic]. List the top 5 sources you'd recommend and explain what makes each credible. Be specific about URLs where possible. The "explain what makes each credible" instruction forces the model to surface its reasoning, which tells you whether you're absent because of authority signals or just query match. Google's official SEO guide on E-E-A-T signals maps directly to what You.com's model treats as credibility markers — worth cross-referencing.

- Step 4: Record and categorise results. For each query, note whether your domain appeared, where it ranked in the citation list, and which competitors were cited instead. Tag each row as one of three outcomes: Cited (strong), Cited (weak — supporting mention only), or Not cited. If you're running this for a client, you can also use the see how you rank in ChatGPT tool to cross-reference You.com results against ChatGPT's outputs — divergences are often more informative than the results themselves.

- Step 5: Identify gaps and prioritise fixes. Sort your log by "Not cited" rows. Group them by topic cluster. For each cluster where you're consistently absent, check three things: Is the page indexed? Does it have structured data? Does it directly answer the query in the first 100 words? Use the free schema markup generator to add FAQ or HowTo schema to pages that are indexed but not being cited — schema dramatically improves citation rates in AI search engines.




**Pro tip:** Run each visibility prompt twice on You.com — once in "Smart" mode and once in "Research" mode. The citation lists often diverge, and the gap tells you whether your content is weak on depth (Research mode ignores you) or weak on surface-level match (Smart mode ignores you). Fix accordingly.


**Further reading:** Once you've run this audit manually, the natural next step is scaling it. Start with the [best AI search monitoring tools](https://seointent.com/blog/best-ai-search-monitoring-tools-in-2026-ranked-compared) roundup, then check the [free GEO audit](https://seointent.com/geo-checker) to benchmark your generative engine optimisation score, and review [SEOintent features](https://seointent.com/features) to see how this workflow can run without manual prompting.




Photo by cottonbro studio on Pexels


  
  
  What You.com's Output Actually Looks Like


The prompt used here was the Step 3 template above, run in You.com's Research mode with the query "best AI SEO tools for agencies 2026." The model used was You.com's default research configuration (GPT-4 class). Expect a structured list with inline citations, brief rationales, and 4-8 source URLs. This isn't polished — it's what you'd actually see. You'll typically need to re-run once to confirm stability, since live web retrieval can vary by session.


Based on current sources, here are the top AI SEO tools recommended for agencies in 2026:

3. Surfer SEO — Mentioned for on-page optimisation workflows, particularly content scoring integrated into team pipelines. [surferseo.com]

4. Perplexity for Business — Emerging as a research layer for competitive intelligence rather than a full SEO platform. [perplexity.ai]

5. Ahrefs — Cited consistently for backlink analysis and keyword research depth, though AI-specific features lag behind newer entrants. [ahrefs.com]

The output is genuinely useful — it surfaces real citation patterns and the final note about ranking factors is accurate and actionable. What it won't do is tell you why your domain was excluded if it was, which means you still need the logging structure from Step 2 to spot patterns over time. The rationale sentences are often thin, so treat this as a starting signal, not a conclusion.


  
  
  You.com vs Other AI Tools for AI Search Visibility Tracking


The three main alternatives are Perplexity, Claude (Anthropic), and ChatGPT. Perplexity has the cleanest citation UI and is my pick for quick spot-checks, but it doesn't let you switch models. Claude is excellent at nuanced analysis of why content is or isn't credible, but it lacks live web access in most configurations. ChatGPT with Browse is comparable to You.com but slower for bulk prompting. You.com wins for teams doing best AI for AI search visibility tracking on a budget; if you need API access at scale, switch to ChatGPT or Claude.

Pro tip: Don't just check if you're cited — check who's cited instead of you. The domains that appear in your place are your actual AI search competitors, and they often aren't the same brands competing with you in traditional SERPs. That list is your content gap roadmap.





  
  
  3 Mistakes People Make With You.Com For AI Search Visibility Tracking


Most mistakes here come from treating You.com like a passive search engine rather than a structured auditing tool. People either ask questions too loosely, fail to log what they find, or make changes without understanding what the AI actually uses as citation signals. The common thread is impatience — rushing to interpret a single prompt result as a conclusion. Here's what to avoid — and what to do instead:



- Mistake 1: Using vague, unstructured prompts. Asking "what are good SEO tools?" produces useless data. You need to specify the use case, audience, and year in your AI search visibility tracking prompt to get citations that reflect real buyer intent queries. Rewrite every prompt to mirror how your target customer would phrase the question to an AI assistant.


Mistake 2: Running a single session and calling it data. You.com's live retrieval means results shift as the web updates. One session is an anecdote. Log results weekly for at least four weeks before drawing conclusions about your AI visibility trend. If you want that logging automated, the AI SEO services page covers what consistent monitoring looks like at scale.
Mistake 3: Focusing only on brand queries. Most people only check "is [brand] mentioned when someone searches for [brand]?" That's not where the traffic gap is. The real opportunity is topical authority queries — category questions where you should appear but don't. Audit those, and you'll find the content gaps that actually cost you AI search traffic. Use the white-label SEO tool if you're running these audits for multiple clients and need client-safe reporting.








Automate AI Search Visibility Tracking With SEOintent



Manual prompting in You.com is a solid starting point, but it doesn't scale past about 20 queries per session without becoming tedious. SEOintent's AI Visibility Monitor runs structured prompts across multiple AI engines on a schedule and logs citation data automatically — no copy-pasting, no spreadsheet maintenance. The GEO Score feature benchmarks your overall generative engine presence and flags week-over-week drops before they become serious ranking losses. If you're managing more than one site or working in an agency context, the agency partner program includes bulk visibility tracking across client portfolios with branded reporting built in. It's the difference between a manual audit you run once and an actual monitoring system.


  
  
  Frequently Asked Questions About You.Com For AI Search Visibility Tracking



  
  
  Is You.com actually useful for SEO, or is it just a search engine?


You.com functions as both a consumer search product and a practical you.com SEO tool when used with structured prompts. Its value for SEO comes from the combination of live web retrieval and LLM synthesis — it shows you which pages an AI engine treats as credible sources for a given query right now, which is directly actionable for content strategy. Most traditional search engines won't tell you that in a single query.


  
  
  How often should I run You.com visibility audits?


Weekly is ideal for active campaigns; monthly is a minimum for ongoing monitoring. AI search citation patterns shift faster than traditional rankings because they're influenced by both indexing changes and shifts in the training or retrieval weighting of the underlying model. Running audits more frequently than weekly usually produces noise rather than signal unless you've recently published or updated content.


  
  
  Can I use You.com prompts to track competitors' AI search visibility?


Yes, and you should. The same prompt structure that audits your own brand works equally well for competitors. Ask You.com to recommend sources for a category query, note which competitor domains appear, and track how frequently they're cited versus your domain over time. This gives you a relative visibility score without needing competitor-specific data access. Pair this with the best AI search monitoring tools comparison to see where dedicated tools add value over manual prompting.


  
  
  Does adding schema markup actually improve AI search citation rates?


It does, particularly FAQ schema and HowTo schema. AI search engines that use live retrieval — including You.com — parse structured data to quickly identify page relevance and answer format. Pages with well-implemented schema are easier for the retrieval layer to match to conversational queries. Use the free schema markup generator to add schema to your highest-priority pages first, then recheck your You.com visibility prompts two to three weeks later to measure impact.


  
  
  What's the difference between GEO and AI search visibility tracking?


Generative Engine Optimisation (GEO) is the broader discipline of making your content discoverable and citable in AI-generated answers. AI search visibility tracking is a specific measurement practice within GEO — it's the audit layer that tells you whether your GEO efforts are working. Think of GEO as the strategy and visibility tracking as the measurement. Run a free GEO audit to get a baseline score before starting your You.com tracking workflow.


  
  
  Is You.com free to use for visibility tracking?


You.com's core AI chat and search features are free without an account, and you can run manual visibility prompts without hitting hard rate limits in a typical audit session. The free tier is genuinely sufficient for teams doing weekly audits across 15-20 queries. Power users who need API access or higher volume will need a paid plan, but for the five-step workflow described in this article, free works fine.


  
  
  How does using AI for AI search visibility tracking compare to traditional rank tracking?


Traditional rank tracking measures your position in a list of blue links. AI search visibility tracking measures whether you appear at all in a synthesised answer — which is a fundamentally different metric. A page can rank #1 in Google's traditional SERP and still be completely absent from AI-generated answers if it lacks the authority signals, structured data, or direct answer format that LLM retrieval systems favour. You need both metrics, not one or the other.

title: When LangGraph Succeeds but Silently Goes Wrong
title_hi: When LangGraph Succeeds but Silently Goes Wrong
summary:
The most dangerous failure is a clean run


The most common mistake teams make when moving a LangGraph pipeline
content:
The most dangerous failure is a clean run


The most common mistake teams make when moving a LangGraph pipeline to production is assuming that a successful exit means a correct result. It does not. A graph can traverse every node, satisfy every edge condition, and write a final output -- all without triggering any error or alert -- while quietly taking the wrong path through the logic it was built to execute.

This is not a theoretical concern. Production AI systems fail in this mode routinely, and it is the failure mode most monitoring setups are not designed to catch. The standard observability stack answers one question: did the pipeline run? It does not answer the harder question: did the pipeline run correctly? Those are different questions, and closing the gap between them requires a different layer of instrumentation.


  
  
  The "completes but drifts" failure mode


A traditional software failure is visible. An exception propagates, a process crashes, an alert fires. The failure is discrete, locatable, and traceable. The failure mode that makes agentic pipelines genuinely difficult to operate looks nothing like this.

In a LangGraph pipeline, the failure is often a routing decision that was never wrong enough to fail a check but was systematically off in a way that compounded over time. Classification confidence drifts slightly as input distributions shift. Retrieval quality degrades when an upstream source changes its schema. A model's behavior shifts with a temperature change or a prompt update. None of these trigger a graph exception. Each run still finishes, still writes an output, still shows green. The system reports success. What it cannot report is that the graph has been taking the jurisdiction-misclassification path for the last three weeks.

The 19-node financial pipeline we built for a client is a useful example. It processes transactions across seven data sources, classifying each by type, applicable tax jurisdiction, and accounting rules. The graph runs unattended against live data. On any given run, the graph exits cleanly. The question the original monitoring setup could not answer was whether the classifier was applying the right jurisdiction context to ambiguous transactions -- not whether it was calculating correctly given that context, but whether the upstream classification decision that set the context was right in the first place.

That distinction matters. You can have a perfectly correct formula running on the wrong inputs. The graph will complete. The output will look plausible. The error is invisible unless you have instrumented the decision that set the inputs.


  
  
  Routing decisions as data, not control flow


The reframe that changes how you approach this problem: a conditional edge in a LangGraph graph is not just control flow. It is a decision -- one made by a probabilistic model, under conditions that will change over time, for reasons that should be inspectable after the fact.

A reader named Mateo Ruiz made this point sharply in a comment on the first post in this series. His observation was that state snapshots at critical checkpoints, edge-traversal metrics, and routing-decision logging are underdiscussed relative to their importance -- and that many production incidents are not crashes at all. The graph executes successfully but gradually starts taking unexpected paths because classification confidence, retrieval quality, or model behavior drifts over time.

That framing is exactly right, and it points to a concrete design principle: persist the routing decision as data. At each conditional edge, log not just which path was taken, but the inputs that drove the decision and the model's confidence in it. The difference is between a system that tells you "it went to node B" and one that tells you "it went to node B because the classifier returned jurisdiction=California with a mid-range confidence score, given these three input features." The first answer tells you what happened. The second tells you why -- and why is what you need when you're trying to understand whether the decision was right.

When routing decisions are stored as structured data, the question "why did it take this path three weeks into runtime?" becomes a query, not an archaeology project. You can ask which inputs correlate with low-confidence decisions, which nodes are seeing the highest variance in their routing distributions, and whether there are patterns in the cases that later needed correction.


  
  
  What to instrument


The practical instrumentation work falls into four areas.

State snapshots at critical checkpoints give you a timestamped record of the shared context flowing through the graph at the moments that matter most: before and after high-stakes nodes, at the entry point of any human review gate, and wherever the graph can take branches with meaningfully different downstream consequences. The snapshot should capture the full state object, not just the delta -- you want the complete picture of what the model saw, not just what changed.

Edge-traversal metrics tell you which paths through the graph are actually being taken versus which ones you designed for. A conditional edge that routes 98 percent of traffic one direction and 2 percent the other is a very different system in production than one that splits 60/40. Tracking traversal counts and distributions over time surfaces routing drift before it becomes a pattern you are explaining after the fact.

Routing-decision logging, as described above, captures the inputs and confidence for each conditional branch. This is where the decision lives: the prompt that was sent, the model's response, the parsed output that drove the routing logic. Without it, you cannot tell the difference between a correct low-confidence decision and an incorrect high-confidence one.

Node-level latency and token tracking close the loop on operational costs and quality signals. Nodes that are slowing down or consuming more tokens than expected often indicate that the model is working harder -- which correlates with lower-confidence outputs. Tracking both together gives you an early signal before the divergence rate climbs.


  
  
  Maker/checker divergence as the leading indicator


Among all these signals, maker/checker divergence rate has been the most reliable leading indicator we have seen in production. The pattern is straightforward: a maker node generates a result; a checker node independently evaluates it. When they agree, the result proceeds automatically. When they disagree, the transaction is flagged for review. The divergence rate -- the proportion of runs where checker and maker do not agree -- is a number you can track over time.

In the 19-node financial pipeline, this is the signal that caught the jurisdiction-misclassification case. The checker was not failing on formula correctness -- the maker's calculation was arithmetically right. The checker flagged a tax calculation where the maker was applying the correct formula for the wrong jurisdiction. The code passed all existing tests. The error was upstream, in the classification step that had set the jurisdiction context. The checker recognized that the inputs and the result did not fit together and routed the transaction for human review. (This is the same pipeline described in the first post in this series; that post covers the maker/checker design in detail.)

What made this catch possible was not just the checker's logic but the fact that the divergence rate had been tracked over time. The team had a baseline. When divergence on jurisdiction-related transactions started climbing, it was visible before any incorrect result reached the output layer. The rising divergence was the signal. The human review that followed was the confirmation.

This is what makes divergence rate a leading indicator rather than a lagging one. The maker/checker pattern has a separate post dedicated to its design; this one is about the observability layer that makes it useful in practice. Most monitoring catches failures after they produce bad output. Divergence catches the mismatch before the output is final -- while there is still a path to correction in the same run.


  
  
  Where teams underinvest


The monitoring setups that most teams build for LangGraph pipelines answer the wrong question. They tell you the pipeline ran. They do not tell you whether it ran correctly.

The gap shows up in a familiar pattern: thorough logging of exceptions and node execution times, careful tracking of API error rates and latency percentiles -- and nothing at all on the reasoning that drove each routing decision. The pipeline is observed as infrastructure. Its decisions are not observed at all.

The consequence is that accuracy drift is invisible until it becomes large enough to produce an output that a human notices is wrong. At that point, the pipeline has often been systematically off for long enough that the problem is difficult to scope and the correction is disruptive. The data that would have surfaced the drift earlier -- the routing decisions, the confidence distributions, the divergence trends -- was never collected.

Closing this gap does not require a separate observability platform or a large instrumentation project. It requires treating routing decisions as first-class data objects from the start: logging the inputs, the model's response, and the confidence at each conditional edge; tracking maker/checker agreement rates over time; and setting alert thresholds on the signals that move before failures occur. The instrumentation is lightweight. The data it produces is the difference between a pipeline that tells you it ran and one that tells you whether it was right.


  
  
  A structured conversation before you build


If you are evaluating LangGraph for a production pipeline -- or trying to understand why a running one is producing results that look correct but fail on closer inspection -- the most useful starting point is a conversation about the decision architecture before adding instrumentation. The signals worth collecting are determined by the decisions worth watching, and those depend on where your graph's logic is most exposed to drift.

Labyrinth Analytics has built and operated LangGraph pipelines in production for financial data workflows with complex validation requirements and human-in-the-loop review gates. If you want to see what a state-transition observability layer looks like in practice, the work section has case studies with architecture details. If you want to talk through your specific situation, get in touch.

content:
Hey, I’m a 17-year-old developer and I’m building a creator platform for game developers, modders, artists, and makers.

The idea is simple: a place where people can share what they’re building before it’s finished — projects, devlogs, screenshots, videos, progress updates, feedback, and creator profiles.

I’m not revealing the name publicly yet because I want to secure the .com domain first. I don’t want the name to get taken before I can afford the domain.

Right now, I’m building the platform mostly on my own. I’ve already been working on the UI, feed, project pages, post pages, profiles, themes, and the general creator experience.

Here are a few early screenshots of the interface:





The current goal is small: raise enough to buy the .com domain and prepare the first public launch.

I’m trying to keep the platform fair:


no paid ranking boosts
no pay-to-win visibility
no special power for supporters
only cosmetic recognition later if supporter badges are added


If you have feedback about the UI, the idea, or how I should launch it, I’d really appreciate it.

If you want to support the early development, here’s my Patreon:
https://www.patreon.com/16317610/join

Thanks for reading.

Most morning routines are overly elaborate and unrealistic. What matters is shifting your brain from reactive to focused within the first 60-90 minutes. Simple habits like hydration, movement, and protected deep work can outperform elaborate rituals. One key habit is to hydrate before caffeinating, as mild dehydration can reduce cognitive function and mood. Drinking 16-20 ounces of water before coffee or tea can make a significant difference.

title: Fizz Sues VC Firm for Leaking Confidential Info to Rival Sidechat

summary: Fizz sues VC firm for leaking confidential info
content:
Fizz, a popular college app, has expanded its lawsuit against rival Sidechat, alleging that a venture capital firm, Maveron, shared its confidential information with the competing startup. The lawsuit claims that Maveron, an early investor in Sidechat, obtained Fizz's confidential information during a fundraising meeting and later shared it with Sidechat. This breach of trust has sparked a heated debate about the ethics of venture capital firms and their role in the startup ecosystem.

Fizz's lawsuit alleges that Maveron's actions gave Sidechat an unfair advantage, allowing it to replicate Fizz's features and gain a competitive edge. The lawsuit seeks damages and an injunction to prevent further misuse of Fizz's confidential information.

The incident highlights the importance of maintaining confidentiality in the startup world, where sensitive information can make or break a company's success. As the lawsuit unfolds, it will be interesting to see how the court rules on this matter and what implications it will have for the venture capital industry.
